coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots synthesized from acrylic acid and ethylenediamine for simple and selective determination of cobalt ions in aqueous media
coredata.dc:creator: Jing, Na
Tian, Min
Wang, Yingte
Zhang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with a quantum yield of 22.7% and an average size of about 4.28 nm were hydrothermally synthesized using acrylic acid and ethylenediamine in one pot. When excited at 350 nm, the NCDs showed a strong emission peak centered at 425 nm, corresponding to blue fluorescence. Interestingly, for NCDs, significant decrease in fluorescence was observed upon addition of cobalt ions (Co2+). On the basis of this particular phenomenon, the synthesized NCDs are demonstrated as an effective fluorescence probe for the selective and quantitative analysis of low concentration Co2+ in a linear range of 1.0–60 µM. Limit of detection (LOD) is 0.25 µM. Further detailed investigations on the interactions of NCDs with Co2+ as quencher by using various techniques (UV–vis absorption and time-resolved fluorescence techniques) confirmed that the fluorescence quenching is mainly induced by the interaction of between Co2+ and some functional groups of NCDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Acrylic acid
Ethylenediamine
Fluorescence
Cobalt ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of NCDs detection of Co2+
Determination of detection limit of Co2+
Possible response mechanism of NCDs towards Co2+
Determination of Co2+ in real water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support for this work from Shanxi Scholarship Council of China , China ( 2015-001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) size distributions images of NCDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of the NCDs. (b) C1S. (c) N1S. (d) O1S spectrum of the NCDs.

(a) The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of NCDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs under different excitation wavelength. Inset: photograph of NCDs aqueous solution under visible light (left) and UV light (365 nm) (right).

(a) Effect of different metal ions on F/F0 solution at 425 nm. (b) Fluorescence response of the NCDs aqueous solution at 425 nm in the presence of different metal ions with (red sign) and without (black sign) Co2+ (pH 7.0, 0.01 M PBS, λex=350 nm, [Mn+]=60 µM [ Co2+]=20 µM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of NCDs solution in the presence of various concentration of Co2+ (0–200 µM). (b) The linear relationship curve between F0/F and the concentration of Co2+ (F0 and F represent fluorescence intensities at 425 nm in the absence and presence of Co2+).

(a) Fluorescence decay of NCDs in the absence of 50 µM Co2+. (b) Fluorescence decay of NCDs in the presence of 50 µM Co2+. (c) The UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs in the absence and presence of 50 µM Co2+. (d) The amplified UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs in the absence and presence of 50 µM Co2+.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of NCDs and its application for Co2+ detection.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of Co2+.
Recovery tests of spiked Co2+ in tap water measured with NCDs.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots synthesized from acrylic acid and ethylenediamine for simple and selective determination of cobalt ions in aqueous media

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, 92 Wu Cheng Road, Taiyuan 030006, Shanxi Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Na Jing and Min Tian contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with a quantum yield of 22.7% and an average size of about 4.28 nm were hydrothermally synthesized using acrylic acid and ethylenediamine in one pot. When excited at 350 nm, the NCDs showed a strong emission peak centered at 425 nm, corresponding to blue fluorescence. Interestingly, for NCDs, significant decrease in fluorescence was observed upon addition of cobalt ions (Co2+). On the basis of this particular phenomenon, the synthesized NCDs are demonstrated as an effective fluorescence probe for the selective and quantitative analysis of low concentration Co2+ in a linear range of 1.0–60 µM. Limit of detection (LOD) is 0.25 µM. Further detailed investigations on the interactions of NCDs with Co2+ as quencher by using various techniques (UV–vis absorption and time-resolved fluorescence techniques) confirmed that the fluorescence quenching is mainly induced by the interaction of between Co2+ and some functional groups of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt is well-known as a trace element in nature, and is beneficial to the organism. But excessive intake of this element can lead to asthma, rhinitis, allergic dermatitis vasodilation and cardiomyopathy in human and animals [1,2]. In view of the toxic effect caused by Co2+, the maximum acceptable level of Co2+ in drinking water is permitted to be 40 µg L−1 (World Health Organization) [3]. Consequently, finding simple and effective methods for quantifying and detecting Co2+ in aqueous solution at low concentration is of great significance.

At present, several principal analytical methods including UV–vis absorbance [4], flame atomic absorption spectrometry [5], ICP-AES [6], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [7], chemiluminescence [8] and electrochemical methods [9] have emerged aiming to monitor Co2+. However, these techniques usually require either expensive equipment, cumbersome processes or tedious pretreatments, making them difficult to apply for the real-time determination of Co2+. Fluorescence probe has been evolving as a promising candidate for Co2+ detection by virtue of its high specificity towards target substances, great simplicity and high-speed analysis during experiments processes [10–12]. So far, some nanoscale fluorescent materials such as organic nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots and nanocrystals have been studied and designed as fluorescence probes for Co2+ detection, but a number of issues associated with these materials, like notable toxicity, low bio-compatibility and complicated synthesis, may hinder their specific use in related fields. Therefore, searching for a kind of novel material with similar properties is needed for replacing above-mentioned materials.

Carbon nanodots (CDs) are an emerging class of carbon-based nanomaterials with small sizes (usually smaller than 10 nm) and have become one of the most popular fluorescence nanomaterials in recent years. Owing to their unique optical properties and low toxicity [13], excellent biocompatibility [14], tunable surface functionalities [15], favorable chemical stability [16] and etc, CDs have become a most potential substitute for other conventional nanoscale fluorescence materials (organic dyes or semiconductor quantum dots), and developed as probes with excellent performance for selective sensing a variety of metal ions. In addition, CDs have also shown great potential in the fields of photo/electrocatalysis [17], bioimaging [18,19], drug and gene delivery [20] and sensors [21–26]. CDs with differential properties and structures can be easily and inexpensively acquired by distinct synthesis approaches which could be classified into two broad synthesis categories: top-down synthesis (thermal oxidation, laser ablation and etc) and bottom-up synthesis (microwave assisted passivation, hydrothermal method and etc). Hydrothermal method [27] can be regarded as a simple and low cost approach to access CDs on a large scale. Research found that the quantum yield of CDs could be improved effectively by doping N atoms, which has been recognized as a valid strategy that could effectively regulate and improve their intrinsic properties [28].

In the present work, NCDs produced from two precursors, acrylic acid and ethylenediamine, have been successfully synthesized by a straightforward and quick hydrothermal method and characterized using various spectrometers. More importantly, on the basis of the significant fluorescence quenching of NCDs resulted from Co2+, the sensor proposed here can be applied for selectively sensing Co2+ over other metal ions. Furthermore, data from recovery experiment of Co2+ in tap water indicated the described method in this paper is reliable and feasible for Co2+ determination in water samples.

Acrylic acid, ethylenediamine, Cr2(SO4)3·6H2O, KNO3, LiCl·H2O, CoSO4·7H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Pb(NO3)2, NaNO3, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, CdSO4·8H2O, Ba(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, MnSO4·H2O, Ag2SO4, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, FeSO4, FeCl3·6H2O, HgCl2, NaCl, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, Quinine sulfate. All chemical reagents used in the experiments were of analytical grade. Dialysis bag (1000 Da) was obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company. Tap water samples were supplied from our laboratory. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used throughout the entire experiments.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs under different conditions were measured at the room temperature on a Shimadzu Corporation RF-5301 Spectrophotometer with xenon lamp as the excitation source. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of the NCDs were recorded at room temperature on a Shimadzu Corporation UV-2450 Spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the NCDs were recorded with a Nicolet iS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The fluorescence lifetimes of the NCDs without and with addition of Co2+ were measured and recorded by PTI QuantaMaster™400 and PicoMaster 1000-TCSPC spectrofluorometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images can be collected using a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope to illustrate the morphology, microstructure and size distribution of these NCDs.

For the synthesis of NCDs, acrylic acid (2.68 mL), ethylenediamine (2.1 mL) as precursors and 50 mL deionized water were mixed fully and transfered into 100 mL reaction kettle, and then heated in an oven (200 °C, 5 h). The obtained orange-yellow reaction production, allowed for cooling down to room temperature, was filtered using 0.15 µm filter paper and then dialyzed for 24 h using dialysis bag (weight cutoff: 1000 Da) to purify the NCDs solution. Finally, the pure NCDs solution was stored in the refrigerator for further research.

The specific recognition of Co2+ by the NCDs was carried out in PBS buffer solution (pH=7.0) by the fluorescence spectroscopy. The specific operation is as follows: firstly, Co2+ with known concentration were added to NCDs solution diluted 200 times by PBS buffer solution to form mixed solution, which were then transferred into 50 mL volumetric flask. Next, 2 mL NCDs solution mixed perfectly with various concentrations of Co2+ were added to the fluorescence cuvette and measured when excited at 350 nm. Finally, we recorded their respective fluorescence intensities under maximum emission wavelength (425 nm). All the parameters kept same during the measurement process.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images can be collected to gain further insight into the morphology of NCDs particles. We could easily observe that the NCDs were well dispersed with an approximately round shape from TEM image ( Fig. 1a). These nanoparticles displayed an uniform distribution, as evidenced by spacing distribution (Fig. 1b), demonstrating an average size 4.28 nm.

To understand the molecular character of the NCDs, fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was performed to characterize the surface functional groups existed on the synthesized NCDs samples. From this spectrum shown in Fig. 2, specifically, the band in the region of 2800–3300 cm−1 can be potentially assigned to O-H and N-H bending vibrations [29]. The band at 1633 cm−1 and 1552 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C˭O and C˭C bond respectively [30]. The 1398 cm−1 peak is the stretching vibrations of C-N and COO- [31] and the 1044 cm−1 peak confirms a C-O stretch [32].

The surface elemental states of the prepared NCDs were studied by XPS. Three distinct peaks were clearly observed at 294.91, 406.41 and 539.11 eV, attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The result presents that the NCDs are mainly composed of C (57.56%), N (20.92%) and O (21.52%) ( Fig. 3a). The C1s spectrum reveals three peaks at 284.5, 285.3, and 287.6 eV, corresponding to C˭C, C-N, C˭O, respectively (Fig. 3b). The two peaks of 398.1 eV and 399.6 eV in the N1s spectra shown in Fig. 3c can be attributed to N-H and C-N, respectively. The O1s spectra exhibits three peaks at 530.2, 530.9 and 531.6 eV, which are related to C-OH, C-O and COO-, respectively (Fig. 3d). Together, based on FTIR and XPS analysis, we confirm that the nitrogen (N) doping was successful and NCDs surface were coated with amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl, which contributed to enhance hydrophilicity of NCDs. Our better understanding of the composition of NCDs surface will help to further analyse fluorescence behavior of the obtained NCDs.

The optical properties the NCDs were examined using UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. There are two strong absorption peaks in the UV–vis absorption spectrum ( Fig. 4a) with λmax values of ~238 nm and ~318 nm, which could be assigned to π-π* transition (C˭C) [33] and n-π* transition (C˭O) [34] respectively. The fluorescence spectra of NCDs, acquired under 350 nm excitation, feature a emission peak with a maximum at 425 nm. The inset shows photographs of NCDs solutions under day light (left) and UV illumination (right).

The fluorescence emission peak red shifts from 410 nm when the excitation wavelength was adjusted from 310 nm to 430 nm, visualized in Fig. 4b, indicating the typical excitation dependence, which was ascribed to the heterogeneity of functional groups on the surface of the NCDs. These groups generate intermediate states between the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of the NCDs, allowing emission over a wider range of the electromagnetic spectrum [35,36]. Moreover, size effect arising from quantum size may also result in the complexity of the excited state of NCDs [37]. These are in agreement with the observation by other reports previously. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity increased when excitation wavelength increased from 310 nm to 350 nm and decreased when excitation wavelength further increased. The quantum yield (QY) of the resultant NCDs was maintained around 22.7% (Table S1). The fluorescence intensity of CDs produced from acrylic acid with respect to the as-prepared NCDs were rather weak (see Fig. S1), confirming that N atom indeed helps to improve the luminescence of CDs. Moreover, the obtained NCDs exhibited strong fluorescence stability shown in Fig S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of NCDs detection of Co2+
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed sensing system, two control experiments were conducted. In one control experiment, 20 μM Co2+ and 60 µM various metal ions including Ag+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe2+ were added into the solution of NCDs, respectively. And then fluorescence response of NCDs were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 5a, only addition of Co2+ caused a drastic bleaching of the fluorescence emission peaked at 425 nm, suggesting as-prepared NCDs possessed high specificity towards Co2+. Very strong interactions between Co2+ and some surface functional groups (-COOH,-OH, -CONH-) of NCDs contributed to enhance the recognition of NCDs towards Co2+, which could explain the observed decrease in fluorescence intensity.

In another control experiment, 20 µM Co2+ were respectively added into the mixed solution containing of NCDs and 60 µM various metal ions to determine whether the coexisting metal ions have influence on the NCDs detection of Co2+. As shown in Fig. 5b, generally, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs with addition of interference metal ions keeps almost consistent with that of pure NCDs, while the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply once upon addition of Co2+, implying the proposed probe based on NCDs allowed for highly selective determination of Co2+ over other common metal ions. These measurements on along with additional competitive experiment further confirm that the NCDs can be applied to selective monitor Co2+ with high specificity among all coexisting metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of detection limit of Co2+
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs at 425 nm gradually decreased with the progressively increasing concentration of Co2+. Moreover, the intrinsic spectral peak of the system consisted of NCDs and Co2+ kept unchanged with respect to that of NCDs. Detailed relationship of between fluorescence quenching level (F0/F) and the concentration of Co2+ was shown in Fig. 6b. It was observed that the F0/F was directly proportional to Co2+ concentration (1.0–60 µM). Based on the equation: LOD = 3δ/s, where s means slope of this equation and is 0.0085, the LOD of Co2+ was estimated to be 0.25 µM (14.7 µg L−1).

Numerous probes have been employed for Co2+ determination based on different analytical methods, but their performance was distinct, as shown in Table 1 [38–45]. Limit of detection (LOD) of Co2+ is an important parameter used for evaluating analysis performance of various approaches for monitoring of Co2+, that is to say, lower LOD stands for better analysis performance and higher sensitivity for substances. Obviously, the value of LOD in our study is lower than that of those reported Co2+ sensors, indicating the adopted sensor here achieving successfully Co2+ determination with high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of NCDs towards Co2+
ce:section.ce:para:
We introduced the Scheme 1, a possible model, which illustrated the preparation of NCDs as well as the quenching process of NCDs by Co2+. Obviously, the fluorescence of the NCDs was sharply quenched after introducing Co2+ into the NCDs aqueous system. To further explore the mechanism of Co2+ ions sensing, the fluorescence quenching process was measured. Generally, fluorescence quenching can be caused by the static quenching effect (SQE), the dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or by both simultaneously. The SQE and DQE of the ground state complex formation model can be described in terms of Stern-Volmer equation:
F
0
/ F =
K
sv
[ Q ]
+ 1
Where F 0 is the fluorescence intensities without quencher, F is the fluorescence intensities in the presence of quencher, [Q] is the concentration of quencher. In present work, the fluorescence intensity ratio of NCDs (F 0/F) is linear over the Co2+ concentration (F 0/F = 0.0085 C+0.98, R2 =0.996).

In SQE, K sv is interpreted as a ground-state association constant for NCDs and Co2+. However, for DQE, the K sv is part of the following equation:
K
sv
=
K
q
×
τ
0
Wherein, K sv represents Stern-Volmer quenching constant, K q means molecular quenching rate constant, τ
0 is the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs solutions. In the NCDs-Co2+ system, K sv = 0.0085 μM−1 and τ
0 = 5.760 ns, K q was calculated to be 1.47 × 1012 M−1 s−1. This value is much larger because the maximum quenching rate constant of diffusion collision is about 2.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1. Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 7a and Fig. 7b, an insignificant reduction in fluorescence lifetime from 5.760 (τ
0 ) to 5.716 ns (τ) can be observed before and after the addition of Co2+. The value of K q, and slight changes in fluorescence lifetime, demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching induced by Co2+ was probably static quenching [46,47].

The UV–vis absorption spectra were performed to further confirm the above explanation. The absorption peak of NCDs in the presence of Co2+, shown in Fig. 7c and d, shifted from 318 nm to 308 nm, proving that Co2+ changed the electronic structure of NCDs by forming a stable non-fluorescence complex between surface functional groups of NCDs and Co2+, along with energy transfer from the NCDs to the complex [48–50]. These results confirmed that SQE, rather than DQE, could be mainly responsible for the Co2+-induced fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Co2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The highly selectivity and good sensitivity of NCDs towards Co2+ imply that the present fluorescent sensor based on NCDs might be applied for measuring Co2+ in real samples. The practicality of this sensor was evaluated on the basis of recovery of Co2+ measurement in tap water. Specifically, recovery of 103.00%, 100.67% and 99.00% were observed in Table 2 after 5 times parallel determination with lower relative standard deviation (RSD). These data indicate good practicality of this sensor, thus, enabling the NCDs solution to serve as a promising probe for the measurement of Co2+ in real water samples.

In this work, we used acrylic acid and ethylenediamine as carbon sources and nitrogen sources respectively to generate N-doped CDs (NCDs). The synthesized NCDs were employed for assaying Co2+, and a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of NCDs (F0/F) and concentration of Co2+ is established in the range from 1.0 µM to 60 µM. The detection limit was then determined to be 0.25 µM. By exploring in-depth the mechanism of which Co2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of NCDs, we speculated that formation of non-fluorescence NCDs-Co2+ complex from static quenching process of NCDs-Co2+ system is most likely responsible for this phenomenon. It should be possible that Co2+ sensor proposed in this paper could be applied to a wide range of simulation applications including environmental and biological fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support for this work from Shanxi Scholarship Council of China, China (2015-001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.10.059.
